items.question: Who needs feature extraction in a typical ML project?
items.answer: Data scientists and ML engineers building models on text or mixed raw columns need extractors to produce numeric matrices; sklearn Pipeline users rely on Transformers so train and serve apply identical mappings.

items.question: What does a sklearn text vectorizer output?
items.answer: A sparse document-term matrix where each row is a document and each column is a vocabulary token (or hash bucket), with cell values as counts, binary presence, or TF–IDF weights depending on the vectorizer.

items.question: When should you call fit_transform versus transform?
items.answer: Use fit_transform on training data to learn vocabulary or idf and get X_train; use transform only on validation, test, and production data so extractor parameters never relearn from held-out or live samples.

items.question: Where does feature extraction sit relative to cleaning and modeling?
items.answer: After raw inputs are available (often post basic cleaning) and before the estimator: raw text → vectorizer transform → numeric X → classifier fit/predict, usually chained inside one Pipeline object.

items.question: Why wrap vectorizers in a Pipeline?
items.answer: Pipelines guarantee cross-validation refits the vectorizer on training folds only, serialize one artifact for deployment, and prevent accidental refit on test strings that would leak vocabulary statistics.

items.question: How does CountVectorizer differ from TfidfVectorizer at fit time?
items.answer: Both learn a token vocabulary on fit; CountVectorizer stores counts at transform, while TfidfVectorizer additionally computes document frequencies to apply TF–IDF weighting so rare discriminative terms score higher than corpus-wide boilerplate.

items.question: CountVectorizer vs TfidfVectorizer: what changes in the matrix?
items.answer: CountVectorizer outputs non-negative integer term counts (or binary with binary=True). TfidfVectorizer outputs real-valued TF–IDF weights that down-weight tokens appearing in many documents—better for linear models, not for count-based Multinomial NB.

items.question: TfidfVectorizer vs HashingVectorizer: when prefer hashing?
items.answer: TfidfVectorizer stores a vocabulary and supports get_feature_names_out for interpretability. HashingVectorizer uses fixed hash buckets with no stored vocab—prefer it for very large or streaming corpora where memory for every unique token is prohibitive.

items.question: Feature extraction vs feature creation: division of labor?
items.answer: Feature creation adds hand-designed columns from domain rules (tenure buckets, flags). Feature extraction learns automatic mappings from raw fields—especially text vectorizers and encoders—into numeric columns consumed by sklearn estimators.

items.question: Cause: fit_transform is run on the entire dataset including test rows before split. Effect?
items.answer: Test-only tokens enter the vocabulary and idf statistics, validation metrics are optimistically biased, and the saved extractor no longer matches the statistical story of true cold-start deployment on unseen text.

items.question: Cause: max_features caps vocabulary below rare fraud keywords. Effect?
items.answer: Those tokens never receive columns in X, the model cannot weight them, and recall on fraud or abuse classes drops even though overall accuracy on frequent classes looks acceptable.
